conversation.speaker: user
conversation.text: Regarding web security, I used to believe that managing our own authentication via custom OAuth2 implementations and local encrypted user tables was the safest approach.

conversation.speaker: assistant
conversation.text: In-house auth gives you complete control over your authentication flows and user data layout.

conversation.speaker: user
conversation.text: But keeping up with compliance and zero-day exploits is terrifying. I flipped my stance and became convinced that outsourcing authentication entirely to identity-as-a-service third parties like Auth0 or Okta was the only responsible option.

conversation.speaker: assistant
conversation.text: Outsourcing offloads a massive compliance burden and security risk to dedicated experts.

conversation.speaker: user
conversation.text: Then Okta got breached, and our vendor risk management team panicked. I realized third-party dependency is a single point of failure. My definitive belief now is that using open-source, self-hosted identity protocols like Keycloak or Ory Kratos is the optimal strategy for balancing absolute control with proven security standards.

query: What does the user currently believe is the best approach to handling user authentication and identity management?
